coredata.dc:title: Stable nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for selective folate sensing, in vivo imaging and drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Wen, Xiangping
Zhao, Zhonghua
Zhai, Shuqin
Wang, Xiedong
Li, Yingqi
coredata.dc:description: Here, innovative nitrogen and sulfur co-doped bright orange fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are easily obtained using methyl-β-cyclodextrin (Me-β-CD) and methylene blue as original materials via the hydrothermal method. The as-prepared CDs showed excellent fluorescence stability in the wide pH range and external anions and cations, while its fluorescence can be quenched by folate (FA), indicating its sensitivity to FA with a detection limit as low as 0.85 nM. The commendable biocompatibility and superior photobleaching resistance of CDs make it superior to applications in living cell imaging and drug tracking, respectively. Bioimaging of CDs in zebrafish larvae manifests that it is easily circulated to other tissues due to the small size. Moreover, the positively charged CDs exhibits drug loading capabilities of the antitumor drug mitoxantrone (MTO) to achieve a fluorescent nanomedicine system (CDs-MTO) combining tracking and therapy. It was found that the cells exposed to the CDs-MTO showed a significant reduction in numbers by both MTT assay and detection of cell morphology, indicating CDs-MTO has a good ability to inhibit cell growth. The results demonstrate that the CDs can be sensitive to FA and served as a good platform for drug delivery and imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots
Biosensing
Nanocarriers
Imaging
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence stability and bioimaging of CDs
The preparation and characterization of CDs-MTO

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shanxi Science and Technology Development Program
Central Government Guiding Local Science and Technology Development
Shanxi Province “1331 Project” Key Innovation Team Construction Plan Cultivation Team
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21071091 , 21671125 , 21575083 ), the Central Government Guiding Local Science and Technology Development (Grant No. YDZX20191400002477 ), Shanxi Science and Technology Development Program (Grant No. 201903D321105 ), Shanxi Province “1331 Project” Key Innovation Team Construction Plan Cultivation Team ( 2018-CT-1 ), The authors acknowledge instrument test in scientific instrument centre in Shanxi University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of CDs. (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) The distribution histogram of corresponding size of CDs. (c) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs. (d) Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Inset: photographs of CDs under visible (left) and UV light (right, 365 nm).
Characterization of surface functional groups on CDs. (a) Raman spectra of CDs. (b) FTIR spectrum of CDs. XPS spectrum of (c) full scan, (d) C1s, (e) S2p, and (f) N1s of CDs.

The selectivity and sensitivity of CDs to FA. (a) Fluorescence responses of the CDs to FA and other biological molecules in a PBS buffer solution at pH 8.4. (b) Effect of different concentrations of FA (from 0 to 210 μM) on the fluorescence Spectroscopy of CDs. (c) Relationship between intensity F0 F and FA concentration. Inset: The plot of F0 F against FA concentrations from 0 to 5 nM. F0 and F represent the PL intensity of the initial and added FA, respectively.

The determine of Light stability and imaging of CDs in cells and zebrafish larvae. (a) FITC as a control in fixed HeLa cells. (b) CDs in fixed HeLa cells. (c) Fluorescence images of zebrafish larvae injected intravenously with CDs after incubation for 0, 2, 6 h in E3 embryo media. (d) Relationship between the average fluorescence intensity of FITC (a) and CDs (b) in cells and time. (e) Relationship between the average fluorescence intensity and incubation time in zebrafish in Fig. 4c.
The characterization of CDs-MTO. (a) Absorbance spectra of CDs, MTO and CDs-MTO. (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs-MTO. (c) TEM image of CDs-MTO.

Cytotoxicity test of CDs and CDs-MTO. (a) CDs and (b) CDs-MTO and free MTO by MTT assay for 24 h (Inset: Cell viability of CDs-MTO and MTO-treated cells at different concentrations). (c) Morphology of cells treated with diverse nanoparticles in different time through fluorescence microscopy.

Intracellular accumulation and mechanism. (a) Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of HeLa cells were treated with CDs and CDs-MTO for 1 h. Green and red PL are excited by 488 nm and 543 nm, respectively. Flow cytometry analysis of intracellular fluorescence intensity after incubation of HeLa cells with CDs (b) and CDs-MTO (c) for different time (PE-A represent the green fluorescence intensity). (d) The kinetics of the cellular uptake of CDs and CDs-MTO. (e) Mechanistic research of the uptake of CDs-MTO under different experimental conditions. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of CDs by hydrothermal process.

head.ce:title: Stable nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for selective folate sensing, in vivo imaging and drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education, Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Chemical Biology and Molecular Engineering of Ministry of Education, Institute of Molecular Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biomedical Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Physics and Electronic Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, innovative nitrogen and sulfur co-doped bright orange fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are easily obtained using methyl-β-cyclodextrin (Me-β-CD) and methylene blue as original materials via the hydrothermal method. The as-prepared CDs showed excellent fluorescence stability in the wide pH range and external anions and cations, while its fluorescence can be quenched by folate (FA), indicating its sensitivity to FA with a detection limit as low as 0.85 nM. The commendable biocompatibility and superior photobleaching resistance of CDs make it superior to applications in living cell imaging and drug tracking, respectively. Bioimaging of CDs in zebrafish larvae manifests that it is easily circulated to other tissues due to the small size. Moreover, the positively charged CDs exhibits drug loading capabilities of the antitumor drug mitoxantrone (MTO) to achieve a fluorescent nanomedicine system (CDs-MTO) combining tracking and therapy. It was found that the cells exposed to the CDs-MTO showed a significant reduction in numbers by both MTT assay and detection of cell morphology, indicating CDs-MTO has a good ability to inhibit cell growth. The results demonstrate that the CDs can be sensitive to FA and served as a good platform for drug delivery and imaging.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para: As a newcomer of the carbon nanomaterial family, CDs have shown a considerable number of applications, such as photoelectric conversion [1], photocatalysis [2], ink [3], drug delivery [4] and bioimaging [5]. In recent years, CDs has been found to hold loads of excellent properties including high optical absorption, chemical inertness, hypotoxicity, good biocompatibility, easy functionalization, anti-photobleaching [6,7] and a wide range of excitation and emission spectra as multicolor labeling and detection [8–11]. Although they possess many fascinating luminescent properties, most of them display strong radiation at blue or green wavelengths [12–14]. While CDs that emits a long-wavelength is vital for bioimaging. In order to gain such a goal, surface modification of CDs has been implemented [15,16], but it is complicated and time-consuming processes. Nowadays, nitrogen (N) atoms [17–20] and the larger atoms like P or S-doped CDs are getting more and more attention because they can effectively tune the intrinsic properties of CDs, including their surface chemical reactivities, electronic properties, and optical properties [21–23] and give them excellent performance in electrochemical and drug delivery [24–26], thus heteroatom-doped CDs are very desirable.

Folate (FA), also known as vitamin B9, is a water-soluble vitamin that promotes the maturation of young cells in the bone marrow. FA participates in many physiological processes in the body, but it cannot be synthesized by itself and can only be exogenously absorbed through the intestine. 0.4 mg FA is used to prevent fetal neural tube defects. The lack of FA in humans can cause macrocytic anemia and leukopenia, especially for pregnant women. Hence, it is extremely necessary to timely and effectively detect the FA content in the human body, food and medicine. Some methods for detecting FA have been established [27–29], but most of them have disadvantages such as expensive instruments, long detection time, and poor selectivity and sensitivity. So it is very significant to develop a low consumption, rapid, highly selective and sensitive means for detecting FA.

Now, cancer has become a serious health issue across the world. So far, about 80 anticancer drugs have been used clinically [30–33]. However, most of chemotherapeutics have severe side effects to limit their applications. For example, mitoxantrone, an anthracycline antibiotic highly effective against various human cancers, has been extensively applied for the treatment of malignant lymphoma, breast cancer and acute leukemia. But mitoxantrone brings some disadvantages of moderate myelosuppression and cardiotoxicity during the cancer treatment. Moreover, its own weak fluorescence or self-annihilation fluorescence makes it impossible to trace the distribution of drugs in cells or in vivo. With the continuous progress of nanotechnology, nanoparticles have been diffusely used as carriers for antitumor drugs [34–36]. Compared with other nanomaterials, CDs can be used as potential candidates for drug nanocarrier and drug delivery. As best as we known, there have been some reports on the use of CDs as drug carriers and theranostic systems [37–39]. For example, Gu et al. stated ternary nanoparticles based on peptide dendritic CDs to achieve specific delivery of cancer drugs and high-efficiency cancer treatment [40]. Wu et al. proposed a novel theranostic nanoagent (CQDs@PtPor) via the electrostatic binding between the PtPor and the negatively charged CQDs [41]. However, the emission ranges of these CDs are between 420 and 520 nm, and they can't meet entire requirements of tracking and high efficiency. Hence, it remains a great challenge to acquire CDs with red or NIR fluorescence and high drug loaded.

Here nitrogen and sulfur double-doped and bright orange fluorescent CDs were constructed by hydrothermal treatment of a mixture of methyl-β-cyclodextrin and methylene blue. The as-prepared CDs displayed excellent fluorescence stability and high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of FA. Apart from this, the acquired CDs have good biocompatibility and superior photobleaching resistance compared to the commercial FITC dye. Bioimaging of CDs in zebrafish larvae indicates that it could be used as an excellent candidate for in vivo imaging and tracking. In addition, considering the fluorescence stability and biocompatibility, the CDs is used as a drug carrier to load the antitumor drug mitoxantrone (MTO) through physical adsorption. Thus, a fluorescent nanomedicine system that combined tracking and tumor treatment is obtained. The results demonstrated that the CDs is a promising luminescent material as a fluorescent biomarker for non-fluorescent drug and in vivo imaging and FA sensor.

Methyl-β-cyclodextrin (M-β-CD) was purchased from the Chinese pharmaceutical company Beijing Purchasing and Supply Station. Methylene blue was purchased from China Pharmaceutical Company. NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin and Rhodamine 6G was from E. Merck, Darmstadt. MTT, a mixture of green streptomycin was purchased from Solarbio, Beijing. DMEM was purchased from Thermo Fisher Biological and Chemical Product in Beijing. FBS is the brand of Sijiqing (Hangzhou, China). Trypsin is a product of Sino-American Biotechnology Corporation. Sucrose is available from Amresco Co. (USA). NaN3 was purchased from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Reagent Factory. HeLa cells are offered by the Genetic Engineering Center of Shanxi University.

CDs was manufactured by hydrothermal treatment of methyl-β-cyclodextrin and methylene blue. Specifically, methyl-β-cyclodextrin (0.48 g) and methylene blue (0.16 g) were added to 20 mL DDI water, stirred uniformly, and then the turbid liquid was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor (50 mL) and placed in an electric blast drying oven. The reaction was carried out at 230 °C for 20 h, and then the mix was natural cooled, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 8 min. Finally, it was dialyzed by the membrane with MWCO of 500–1000 Da for 24 h to gain an aqueous solution of CDs. To accurately weigh the CDs, the CDs solution was freeze-dried.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained on a JEM-2100 at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The ZF-7 ultraviolet spectrophotometer produced by Gongyi Kerui Instrument Factory was used for three kinds of operations on the fluorescence photos of CDs under 365 nm ultraviolet light. Raman spectroscopy was measured by a Bruker Senterra dispersive Raman microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was acquired from a shaft ultra DLD electronic spectrometer from Shimadzu Corporation of Japan. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was gotten by employing a FTIR-8400S. The UV–visible absorption spectrum was gained by a Cary 50 Bio UV–vis Spectrophotometer (America). Steady-state fluorescence measurements were performed on a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Australia). The fluorescence lifetime was executed by using the Edinburgh FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY (Φ)) of the CDs was calculated by utilizing the following formula: Φ x = Φ std I x A std η 2 x / (I std A x η 2 std). In the equation, I x and I std represent the fluorescence intensities of CDs and reference, respectively. A x and A std represent the optical densities of CDs and reference, respectively. η x and η std represent the refractive indices of CDs and reference, respectively, in which Rhodamine 6G in ethanol acted as a reference, and QY was 0.94 at 488 nm. Cells imaging was executed under a laser confocal microscope (Leica TCSSP5, Germany) and fluorescence microscope (Zeiss, Vert.A1, Germany).

In order to detect the effect of FA on the fluorescence of CDs, an aqueous solution of 200 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL) was first added to 600 μL of PBS solution with a pH of 8.4, then FA and other small biomolecules were added (200 μg/mL), and the fluorescence intensity was measured. Finally, various concentrations of FA were continuously added. After stirring for 1 min, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was measured and recorded separately. In addition, the same method was used to study the response of common anions and cations, biological molecules and amino acids to the fluorescence intensity of CDs.

Take 1 mg of CDs into a centrifuge tube, add 1 mL of deionized water. After complete dispersion, add 80 μg of MTO into the centrifuge tube, and then place it on a flip-shaker to avoid light for overnight. Next, the reaction liquid was transferred to a dialysis bag with molecular weight of 500–1000 Da and dialyzed against light. During the dialysis process, at the scheduled time, we will continuously replace the ultrapure water outside the dialysis bag so that the system always maintains a certain concentration difference. As time went on, the absorbance of mitoxantrone in the dialysis bag at 609 nm was measured continuously until the absorbance value remained unchanged. According to its standard curve, the final amount of mitoxantrone loaded onto the CDs was 40 ± 3.0 μg/mg.

HeLa cells were seeded in two confocal dishes. After washing twice with PBS, the CDs solution and FITC solution were added, incubated for 20 min, washed twice with PBS, fixed and subjected to confocal imaging. The 488 nm laser is applied to the light source, and the laser intensity is the same, and the scanning mode of continuous laser irradiation is adopted. FITC scan 15 min, CDs were scanned for 60 min to derive an image of the change in fluorescence intensity.

In order to detect the anticancer efficacy of the nanomedicine system, CDs and MTO were used as control groups. The cytotoxicity of CDs-MTO was determined by MTT test. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well culture plates for 16 h, and then treated with different concentrations of CDs, MTO and CDs-MTO for 24 h. After cell treatment, 5 mg·mL−1 of MTT medium continued to incubate for 4–6 h. Finally sucking out the culture liquid, and 150 mL of DMSO solution was joined to dissolve the insoluble formazan crystals. After 10 min, the absorbance at 490 nm was recorded using a microplate reader.

In order to explore the nanodrug uptaken ability by cells, HeLa cells was used as a cell model, and the relationship between the absorption of CDs or CDs-MTO and the time was analyzed by flow cytometry. HeLa cells were seeded in a 35 mm culture plates overnight. Then cells were incubated with the nanoparticles (200 μg/mL CDs) for 10, 25, 40, 60 and 90 min, respectively. After the incubation, the cells were washed with PBS (pH 7.4), and the cells were collected by trypsin digestion. The fluorescence intensity was determined by flow cytometry. The cells without any treatment were the controls.

Next, the cellular uptake mechanism of the nanomedicine was studied, and HeLa cells were seeded in a 35 mm culture plates. Then, the following treatments were carried out: First, the two groups of cells were pretreated at 37 °C and 4 °C for half an hour, respectively, and then HeLa cells were incubated for 2 h by adding CDs-MTO under the same conditions. Another group of cells were pretreated with sodium azide (NaN3, 0.8 M) for 30 min at 37 °C, then CDs-MTO was added for 2 h, and finally washed with PBS (pH 7.4). Cells were trypsinized and collected and analyzed by flow cytometry.

HeLa cells were seeded in a 35 mm culture plates overnight. The CDs or CDs-MTO of concentration of 200 μg/mL suspended in the culture medium were cultivated with cells, respectively. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS, and 300 μL of 4% paraformaldehyde solution was used to fix the cells for 8 min and then washed using PBS. Cells imaging was executed under a laser confocal microscope and fluorescence microscope.

Zebrafish laid eggs under light stimulation, and embryos are collected and washed with standard E3 medium. For the assay of biocompatible CDs, 2 days post-fertilization zebrafish larvae were transferred to a 6-well plate. Zebrafish larvae were intravenously injected with 4 mg/mL CDs, fluorescence pictures were obtained using a laser confocal microscope.

CDs were favourably fabricated by one-step hydrothermal treatment of methyl-β-cyclodextrin and methylene blue (Scheme 1 ). The formation of CDs in hydrothermal systems has been considered as carbonization of precursors, which is recognized a complicated approach involving a series of dehydration and polymerization [42,43]. In this reaction, the hydroxyl-rich methyl-β-cyclodextrin provides a rich carbon source for the formation of CDs, while methylene blue contributes to the functional doping of nitrogen and sulfur in the CDs. The prepared CDs are well dispersible, readily soluble in water, transparent, and emit bright orange fluorescence. However, the CDs prepared with methyl-β-cyclodextrin or methylene blue as raw materials respectively show blue fluorescence (Fig. S1), indicating that both methyl-β-cyclodextrin and methylene blue are indispensable substances for preparing orange CDs with N and S doped.

The size of CDs was measured by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS). As can be seen from the TEM image of Fig. 1a, the CDs are spherical and well dispersed, and the average diameter is about 4.9 ± 0.1 nm (Fig. 1a). However, the DLS information pointed the hydrodynamic diameter as 13.54 ± 0.60 nm in Fig. S2a. The size covered from DLS would always be larger than the actual size of the particle since it indicates the interaction between the solvent and the particle.

The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of the CDs is shown in Fig. 1c, where the emission spectrum gradually shifts from 582 nm to 610 nm with a decrease in PL intensity with increasing excitation wavelength from 470 nm to 550 nm. Obviously, it is typically feature of CDs with excitation wavelength-dependent emission, which may imply two different mechanisms including a size-dependent luminescence and a distribution of emissive trap sites on each CDs [44,45]. Because of its excitation-dependent emission characteristics, CDs can be used for multicolor biological imaging [46,47]. It can be found that the optimum excitation wavelength is 470 nm and the optimum emission peak is at 582 nm (Fig. 1d). Besides, in Fig. 1d the UV–vis absorption spectrum shows a strong absorption peak at 260 nm, which can be ascribe the π–π* transition of the CC bands. The broad peak at ~450 nm is due to the n-π* transition of the CO bands [48]. From the inset in Fig. 1d, we can observe the CDs emit bright orange fluorescence. In addition, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was calculated to be 2.5% using the rhodamine 6G as comparison. (Fig. S3 and Table S2).

Apart from this, Fig. 2a displayed the Raman spectrum of CDs. The peak at 1344 cm−1 belongs to the D band (sp3-hybridized) and is related to the vibration of carbon atoms. The peak at 1520 cm−1 belongs to the G band (sp2-hybridized) and is related with the vibration of the sp2 hybridized carbon atom in the two-dimensional hexagonal lattice [49]. The intensity ratios of the disordered D band to the crystalline G band (I D/I G) is 0.87 for CDs, illustrating that the structure is similar with graphite.

Fig. 2b reveals the FTIR of CDs. The peaks at 3425 and 2987 cm−1 are belong to the OH and CH stretching vibration, peaks at 1708 and 1402 cm−1 ascribed the CO and CN stretching vibration, the peak at 1173 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration, peak at 1609 cm−1 is credited to the CC stretching vibration. Besides, the zeta potential of CDs was determined to be +5.38 mV, which may be mainly caused by the NH2 groups on the surface of CDs.

To further survey the surface groups of the CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectral of CDs was performed. Fig. 2c displayed that the primary elements of CDs involved C, N, O and S elements (in Table S1). Four peaks at 282.2, 399.5, 530.3, and 164.3 eV belong to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p, respectively. The C1s spectrum in Fig. 2d has three peaks at 284.9 eV, 286.2 eV, and 287.7 eV, which are contributed to CC, CN/CO/CS and CO, respectively. The S2p spectrum in Fig. 2e exhibits four peaks at 164.0, 166.2, 168.5, and 170.3 eV, which are contributed to SS, CS, SO, CSOx (x = 2, 3, 4), respectively [50,51]. In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2f), two peaks of 399.7 eV and 401.8 eV belong to CNC and NH. In the O1s of CDs (Fig. S4), 532.6 and 532.7 eV belong to CO and COH/COC. Thus, XPS results indicate that the surface of the CDs contains hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups, and this is consistent with the type of functional groups by FTIR.

To explore its application in sensing, we evaluated the effect of different biological molecules on the fluorescence spectrum of CDs. As shown in Fig. 3a, the CDs show outstanding selectivity for FA. In Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs is significantly decreased as FA concentration increases. The results displayed a good linear relationship in the range of 0–5 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.984 (Fig. 3c). The detection limit of FA calculated is 0.85 nM, which is much lower than previously reported [52,53], indicating that the CDs can be used as a highly sensitive fluorescent probe to quantitatively detect FA.

Next, the mechanism of action between FA and CDs were further studied. As shown in Fig. S5a, the absorption spectra of the FA and the excitation and emission spectra of the CDs were first compared, and it was found that there was no overlap between them, so the energy transfer and internal filter effects were excluded [54]. Further experiments showed that after adding 200 μM of FA to the CDs, the fluorescence lifetime remained basically unchanged (Fig. S5b), which proved that it was the static quenching of the CDs by FA [55]. Next, the effect of fluorescence quenching at different temperatures was explored (Fig. S5c). According to Stern-Volmer equation lg(F 0
F) / F = lgK A + nlg[Q] [56], (F 0
F)-[Q] regression line was done at different temperatures. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching can be judged in term of the relationship between the slope of the straight line and the temperature. If the temperature increases, the slope of the line increases, it is dynamic quenching; otherwise, it is static quenching. As shown in Fig. S5c, as the temperature increases, the slope of the line decreases, further indicating that the quenching process should be a static quenching between the CDs and FA. In order to further clarify the interaction force between the CDs and FA, as displayed in Fig. S5d, the binding constant (KA) between the CDs and the FA can be calculated to 1.83 × 103 mol−1·L (23 °C) and 1.92 × 102 mol−1·L (38 °C). Hence both ΔH = −115 kJ/mol and ΔS = −326 J/(mol·K) were obtained. According to ΔH < 0 and ΔS < 0, indicating that the force between CDs and FA is mainly hydrogen bond and van der Waals force [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence stability and bioimaging of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: For biological applications, it is essential for the fluorescence stability of the CDs. Here the stability of CDs was investigated under various conditions. Firstly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs levelled off in the pH range of 3.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S6a). Secondly, the effects of different anions and cations on the fluorescence of CDs were also detected. Fig. S6b showed that even if the ion concentration is as high as 400 μM, the PL intensity of the CDs emerged minor changes. More importantly, because of photobleaching being one major concerns of CDs nanoparticle, next the photobleaching resistance of CDs was examined. As shown in Fig. 4b, no sign of photobleaching was found when CDs was endocytosis inside HeLa cells even after 1.0 h of continuous excitation with a 488 nm laser. By contrast, FITC (commercial dye) as a control had severe photobleached within 5 min under the same excitation conditions in Fig. 4a. From Fig. 4d, it is clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity of CDs basically remains stable over time, while that of FTIC decreases significantly with time. Hence the as-prepared CDs exhibited excellent photostability, which is a vital feature as fluorescent materials used in real-time tracking intracellular or in vivo processes, especially for long-term imaging.

The merit of photobleaching resistance of CDs makes it possible to detect a long time-imaging in vivo. In the experiment, zebrafish larvae were acted as in vivo model. When the zebrafish larvae were injected intravenously with CDs for 30 min, we observed that obvious green/red fluorescence signal in intravascular. Over time, green/red fluorescence decreased until it disappeared after 6 h incubation (Fig. 4c). It can be intuitively seen from Fig. 4e that the fluorescence intensity in the zebrafish decreases significantly over time until it disappears. Therefore, we speculate that the CDs may be cycled to other tissues along the blood circulates due to the small size. Thus, it is suitable candidate for live bioimaging and tracking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation and characterization of CDs-MTO
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent optical properties, good biocompatibility, rich surface functional groups of CDs make them interesting alternatives as drug nanocarriers, of particularly in order to keep track distribution of no-fluorescence drugs. Here, Mitoxantrone (MTO) as a drug model, was selected to confirm the potential application of CDs as nanocarriers. From Fig. 5a, the typical peak at 609 nm of MTO appeared at the absorption spectrum of CDs-MTO different from free CDs. Besides, Fig. 5c displayed the TEM image of CDs-MTO, most of CDs-MTO falls within the range from 5.3 to 28.6 nm. The average size of CDs-MTO is 15.58 ± 0.21 nm far from the size of free CDs (Fig. 1a), implying the formation of the CDs-MTO complex. Besides, the DLS data provided the hydrodynamic diameter of CDs-MTO is around 58.5 ± 0.60 nm in Fig. S1b, in which the interaction between the solvent and the particles may promote aggregation among CDs-MTO nanoparticles through hydrogen bond, van der Waals forces and π-π interaction. Fig. 5b depicted the PL spectra of CDs-MTO. Compared to CDs, the emission peak of CDs-MTO is slightly blue-shifted and the intensity is a bit reduced, probably fluorescence resonance energy transfer backs up between the CDs and MTO. Apart from these, the zeta potential of CDs changes from +5.38 to +0.52 mV after MTO loading, which was again indicative of surface binding of MTO. MTO is mainly adsorbed on the surface of CDs by hydrogen bonding, van der Waals force and π-π interaction.

The CDs-MTO was examined to assess its latent anticancer effect by MTT assay. When the HeLa cells were treated with various concentrations of CDs or CDs-MTO for 24 h, the cell viability was shown in Fig. 6a-b. The CDs had little effect on the growth of the cells, illustrating that it has good biocompatibility and is suitable as a drug carrier. But CDs-MTO devoted a significant inhibitory effect on cell growth and is a concentration-dependent inhibitory effect. Furthermore, as shown in the Fig. 6b, the IC50 of killing cells by MTO and CDs-MTO were 0.68 and 0.89 μg/mL, respectively, indicating that the presence of CDs does not affect the toxicity of MTO to HeLa cells.

To further detect the cytotoxic effect of nano drug CDs-MTO on cells, the morphology of HeLa cells incubated with CDs-MTO for different periods was observed by fluorescence microscopy, which were demonstrated in Fig. 6c. It was found that the cells exposed to the CDs alone have the same morphology as the control cells and have a good growth state and are full and tangible, indicating that the CDs has low toxicity. While the cells treated with CDs-MTO and free MTO are severely affected, resulting in cell shrinkage, blurry contours and reduced number, and these get worse over time.

Whether CDs and CDs-MTO particles enter living cells, it remains unclear. Next, a microscope was used to record and verify the distribution of nanoparticles in the cells. The HeLa cells were individually incubated with CDs and CDs-MTO at 200 μg·mL−1 for 2 h. As shown in Fig. 7a, after the cells were incubated with CDs, they exhibited evident green and red fluorescence when excited with 488 nm and 547 nm, respectively. However, red fluorescence preferred to the cytoplasm rather than green fluorescence to the nucleus due to the uneven particle size of the nanoparticles. Interestingly, both red and green fluorescence aggregated in cytoplasm not the nucleus when the cells were treated with CDs-MTO. The interesting phenomenon was caused by the increased size of CDs-MTO compared to that of CDs.

Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of intracellular nanoparticles was measured by flow cytometry to indicate the amount of intracellular particles (Fig. 7b and c) [57], and the corresponding change of fluorescence intensity of CDs and CDs-MTO uptake by HeLa cells with time was displayed in Fig. 7d. Compared with the blank, the fluorescence intensity of the cells incubated with CDs and CDs-MTO constantly enhanced with the prolongation of time, and reached saturation in about 1.5 h. The single exponential function curve declared that the rate constants (k) for CDs was about 0.0563 min−1, namely, the half-life was about 12.31 min, while the half-life of CDs-MTO was near 15.37 min. This indicates that the uptake rate of CDs-MTO by cells is slightly slower than that of CD, may be due to the increase in the size of the CDs-MTO.

The side scatter (SS) data of flow cytometry can reflect the complexity of intracellular particles, so the uptake of nanoparticles by cells can be explained by observing the value of SS [58]. Fig. S7 shows the uptake of CDs-MTO by HeLa cells. After incubation at different times, the value of SS stays nearly stable because of small size nanoparticles. In addition, CDs-MTO nanoparticles inside the cells have neglected influence on the cell size from the data of FSC.

Finally, the mechanism of CDs-MTO taken in by cells was carried out. In the assay, cells were treated with the CDs-MTO at a low temperature of 4 °C or in an atmosphere without ATP (NaN3 pretreatment), which has been extensively used as an endocytic inhibitor [59–61]. As can be seen in Fig. 7e, the uptake of CDs-MTO was significantly decreased by >60% at 4 °C. The cell membrane at a low temperature of 4 °C is rigid and in a frozen state, so there is no direct physicochemical translocation between cell membrane and the CDs-MTO, leading to a markedly reduce in its uptake. NaN3 treatment inhibited the uptake of 15% of CDs-MTO. This means that the cellular uptake of CDs-MTO is an energy-independent pathway.

In summary, functionalized nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped bright orange carbon nanodots were friendly obtained by reacting methyl-β-cyclodextrin and methylene blue in water under hydrothermal conditions at 230 °C for 20 h. CDs have excitation wavelength-dependent tuneable fluorescence and can optically monitor cells in both green and red fluorescence channels, which is generally not applicable to organic dyes. Because of the excellent properties of CDs, it can be used as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe to detect FA. Fluorescence imaging of CDs in zebrafish larvae indicates that it is an excellent candidate for bioimaging and tracking. Due to the outstanding biocompatibility, superior photobleaching resistance, CDs could serve as an exciting alternative for in vivo applications. Furthermore, the investigation shows that CDs can be acted as an anticancer drug nanocarrier for mitoxantrone (MTO) to construct a fluorescent nanomedicine system combining tracking and treatment. Cell imaging and morphology assay demonstrate that the nanodrug system can give mitoxantrone fluorescence and does not affect the anticancer effect. Overall, this strategy and application of preparing CDs will help the design of better nanomedicine.

Xiangping Wen: Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Zhonghua Zhao: Software, Formal analysis, Validation. Shuqin Zhai: Formal analysis, Validation. Xiedong Wang: Software, Formal analysis. Yingqi Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest.
